title: Azure Storage Workshop
text:
What this covers
Welcome! This is a short, hands-on workshop for the Imago team on how to get to files we keep in Azure Cloud storage. It’s designed for a mixed audience: you do not need to be a developer to follow along.

title: Azure Storage Workshop
text:
Where our files live in Azure, in plain terms.
The access we’ll hand out to you, and what “read-only” means.
Two ways to get the files, depending on how you like to work.

title: Azure Storage Workshop
text:
Pick your track
There are two parallel tracks. You only need one — pick whichever fits how you like to work. Both get you to the same files.






NoteTrack A — Azure Storage Explorer (no coding)



For everyone who’d rather click than type. A free desktop app from Microsoft that lets you browse our storage like a file explorer and download files with a right-click. → Start Track A








NoteTrack B — Python (azure-storage-blob)



For developers who want to pull files into scripts and pipelines. We’ll use Microsoft’s official Python library to connect, list files, and download them with runnable code. → Start Track B


Before either track, everyone should read the shared Prerequisites & access page.

title: Azure Storage Workshop
text:
A quick note on wording
You may have heard cloud storage described with words like “buckets” and “objects” — that’s the language Amazon (AWS) and Google Cloud use. Azure uses different words for the same ideas:



You might say…
In Azure it’s called…




Bucket
Container


Object / file
Blob


(the overall account)
Storage account



So: our files (blobs) are grouped into containers, which all live inside one storage account. We’ll use the Azure words from here on.

title: Azure Storage Workshop
text:
Scope and ground rules






ImportantThis is a ~1-hour session, and your access is read-only




We’re aiming to get everyone connected and downloading within the hour, so we keep things deliberately simple.
The access we give you is read-only: you can browse and download files, but you cannot upload, change, or delete anything. You don’t have to worry about breaking or overwriting our data — you can’t.
This site doubles as a reference: come back to it any time after the session.



Wherever you see something like PLACEHOLDER-..., that’s a real value (an account name, a container name, your SAS URL) that we’ll give you on the day — it’s left blank here on purpose so we don’t publish secrets.
Ready? Head to Prerequisites & access.

title: Prerequisites & access
text:
How our storage is organised
Read this page before starting either track. It explains the access we give you and where to find the details you’ll paste into Storage Explorer or your code.
Recall the wording from the landing page:
For this workshop you’ll connect to:

title: Prerequisites & access
text:
Files (blobs) are grouped into containers, which live inside one storage account.





Thing
Value (we’ll give you the real one)




Container(s) you can read
data-storage


Example file to download
HP40-2017.tif









Note



The container name above is not secret — it’s just a label. The thing that actually grants access is the token below, which is why we hand that out separately and ask you not to share it.

title: Prerequisites & access
text:
The access we provide
We’ll give you a read-only SAS URL (SAS = Shared Access Signature). It’s the web address of our container with a time-limited key on the end, so it lets you read files without needing an Azure login of your own. It looks like this:
https://PLACEHOLDER-STORAGE-ACCOUNT.blob.core.windows.net/PLACEHOLDER-CONTAINER-NAME?sp=rl&st=YYYY-MM-DDT10:47:56Z&se=YYYY-MM-DDT20:02:56Z&spr=https&sv=YYYY-MM-DD&sr=c&sig=PLACEHOLDER-SIGNATURE






ImportantWhere to find your SAS URL



The SAS URL lives in an internal, Imago-only file — find it here: Imago Azure access details.
This link is restricted to the Imago team; you’ll need to be signed in with your Imago account to open it. Don’t copy the URL anywhere public or share it outside the team.



What the “token” part is
The bit after the ? is the SAS token — the key itself. You don’t need to pull it out separately; the whole URL is what you’ll paste into Storage Explorer (Track A) or your code (Track B). It’s worth recognising the pieces, though:

sp=rl — the permissions: read + list (this is what makes it read-only).
se=... — the expiry date/time. After this, the URL stops working.
sig=... — the signature that actually authorises access. Treat it as secret.



Keep your SAS URL safe
The URL is the key — anyone who has it can read our data until it expires. So:






ImportantTreat your SAS URL like a password




Don’t paste it into emails, chat, screenshots, or commit it to a repo.
For code (Track B), don’t hard-code it — store it in an environment variable (we show how on the setup page) so it never lives in your source.
In Storage Explorer (Track A), the app stores the connection for you — just don’t share screenshots that reveal the URL.
It expires (se= in the URL) — if it stops working, it has probably expired. Ask us for a fresh one.

title: Prerequisites & access
text:
What “read-only” means
Your SAS URL grants read + list permissions only (sp=rl in it — read, list). In practice:



You can
You can’t




See the list of files
Upload new files


Open / preview files
Edit or overwrite files


Download files
Delete files



So you can explore freely — there’s nothing here you can break.

title: Prerequisites & access
text:
What you need installed
This depends on your track. You can sort this during the session, but installing ahead of time means you’ll spend the hour actually using the storage.






TipTrack A — Azure Storage Explorer



A free Microsoft desktop app (Windows, macOS, Linux). Install steps are on the Track A install page.








TipTrack B — Python



Python 3.8+ and the azure-storage-blob package. Setup is on the Track B setup page.

title: Prerequisites & access
section: A note on other auth methods
text:
A note on other auth methods
We’ve chosen a read-only SAS URL because it’s the simplest thing to hand to a group and it’s read-only by design. You may see two other approaches in Azure docs:

Azure AD / Entra ID (DefaultAzureCredential, az login) — more auditable, but needs each person to have an Azure account with a role assigned.
Connection string / account key — grants full account access, so it’s not a good fit for read-only group access.

For this workshop, stick with the SAS URL we provide. Both tracks show where the alternatives would slot in, in case you use them elsewhere.

Got your SAS URL? Pick your track: Track A · Track B.

title: Track A — Azure Storage Explorer
text:
Who this track is for
Anyone who’d rather click than type. If you don’t write code (or just don’t want to for this), this is your track. We’ll use Azure Storage Explorer — a free Microsoft desktop app that shows our cloud storage like a familiar file explorer, with folders to open and files to download.
Developers are welcome here too, but if you’d rather pull files into a script, see Track B.
crumbs: Track A — Azure Storage Explorer (no coding)
3  Track A — Azure Storage Explorer

title: Track A — Azure Storage Explorer
text:
What you’ll do
Three short steps:

Install Storage Explorer — the free Microsoft app.
Connect — paste in the read-only access we gave you.
Browse & download — find files and save them to your computer.
crumbs: Track A — Azure Storage Explorer (no coding)
3  Track A — Azure Storage Explorer

title: Track A — Azure Storage Explorer
text:
Before you start
Make sure you’ve read Prerequisites & access and have your read-only SAS URL.






Note



Remember your access is read-only — you can open and download anything in here, but you can’t change or delete it. Explore freely.


→ Start with Install Storage Explorer.
crumbs: Track A — Azure Storage Explorer (no coding)
3  Track A — Azure Storage Explorer

title: Step 1 — Install Storage Explorer
text:
Download and install
Azure Storage Explorer is a free desktop app from Microsoft. It runs on Windows, macOS, and Linux.
crumbs: Track A — Azure Storage Explorer (no coding)

title: Step 1 — Install Storage Explorer
text:
Go to the official download page: https://azure.microsoft.com/products/storage/storage-explorer
Click the download button. 
Choose the version for your operating system and download the installer. 
Run the installer and follow the prompts — the defaults are fine.
Open Microsoft Azure Storage Explorer once it’s installed.







ImportantIf Storage Explorer won’t start — the .NET 10 fix





Some people hit this error the first time they launch Storage Explorer:

“The authentication library used by Storage Explorer has failed to start properly. Please ensure you have .NET 10 installed. Error: Hub Controller process exited prematurely with exit code 131.”

Why it happens: Storage Explorer needs .NET 10, but it only looks in specific install locations. If .NET was installed via a package manager (Homebrew, mise, etc.) or isn’t installed at all, Storage Explorer can’t find it and fails to start.

Fix for macOS 🍎

Go to https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet/10.0.
Download the .NET Runtime (not the SDK) for macOS.
Pick the matching architecture:

ARM64 → Apple Silicon Macs (M1/M2/M3/M4)
x64 → Intel Macs

Run the Microsoft installer — it places .NET at /usr/local/share/dotnet, which is where Storage Explorer expects it.
Relaunch Azure Storage Explorer.

⚠️ Also check that your Storage Explorer download matches your Mac’s architecture (ARM64 vs x64). The wrong build can stop .NET being detected even when it’s installed correctly.
✅ Confirmed working.


Fix for Windows 🪟

Go to https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet/10.0.
Download the .NET Runtime for Windows (x64 for most systems).
Run the installer.
If it still fails after installing .NET:

Fully uninstall Azure Storage Explorer.
Delete the leftover app-data folder at %AppData%\StorageExplorer.
Reinstall the latest version from https://azure.microsoft.com/products/storage/storage-explorer.

On a corporate machine without admin rights, ask IT to install the .NET 10 Runtime for you.

⚠️ The ASP.NET runtime is not required — the standard .NET Runtime is enough.
crumbs: Track A — Azure Storage Explorer (no coding)

title: Step 1 — Install Storage Explorer
section: What you’ll see on first launch
text:
What you’ll see on first launch
When the app opens you’ll see a Connect dialog (a panel with a power-plug icon) and an empty Explorer pane on the left. That’s expected — we haven’t connected to anything yet.







Tip



You do not need to sign in with a Microsoft account to follow this workshop. We connect using the read-only token we gave you, which doesn’t require a login. If the app prompts you to “Sign in”, you can close that prompt.


→ Next: Connect with your access.
crumbs: Track A — Azure Storage Explorer (no coding)

title: Step 2 — Connect with your access
text:
When it works
Now we’ll tell Storage Explorer how to reach our storage, using the read-only access from Prerequisites & access.
A SAS URL is the container address with the token on the end. It looks like:
Steps:
You’ll see your Display name appear in the left-hand Explorer pane, usually under a section like Local & Attached → Storage Accounts → (SAS-Attached Services) → Blob Containers. Click it to open the container.
→ Next: Browse & download files.
crumbs: Track A — Azure Storage Explorer (no coding)
5  Step 2 — Connect with your access

title: Step 3 — Browse & download files
text:
Browse the container
You’re connected — now let’s find files and save them to your computer.
crumbs: Track A — Azure Storage Explorer (no coding)

title: Step 3 — Browse & download files
text:
In the left Explorer pane, click Storage Accounts and then Blob Containers and select connected container (e.g. data-storage).
The main panel fills with the contents of the container - a list of files and folders.
If you see folder-like entries, double-click to go in. (In Azure these “folders” are really just / in the file names, but they behave like folders here. e.g. Embedding/2017/)







Tip



Use the filter box above the file list to jump to a file by name — handy when a container has lots of blobs. Try typing part of HP40-2017.tif.
crumbs: Track A — Azure Storage Explorer (no coding)

title: Step 3 — Browse & download files
text:
Download a file

Click the file you want (e.g. HP40-2017.tif).
Click Download in the toolbar — or right-click the file and choose Download.
Pick where to save it on your computer, and confirm.
Progress shows in the Activities panel at the bottom; “Transfer successful” means it’s done.

To grab several at once, Ctrl-click (Windows/Linux) or Cmd-click (macOS) to select multiple files, then Download.
crumbs: Track A — Azure Storage Explorer (no coding)

title: Step 3 — Browse & download files
text:
Preview without downloading
Double-click a file to open a preview where supported (text, CSV, images), so you can check you’ve got the right one before downloading.
crumbs: Track A — Azure Storage Explorer (no coding)

title: Step 3 — Browse & download files
section: What you can’t do (and that’s fine)
text:
What you can’t do (and that’s fine)
Because your access is read-only, the Upload, Delete, Rename, and New Folder buttons either won’t appear or will fail with a permissions error. That’s expected — there’s nothing here you can break.






Note



If you genuinely need a file added to the storage, that’s a job for the team who manages it — ask in the Imago channel rather than trying to upload.
crumbs: Track A — Azure Storage Explorer (no coding)

title: Step 3 — Browse & download files
text:
You’re done 🎉
You’ve connected to Azure storage and downloaded a file — no code required. This page stays here as a reference; come back any time.
crumbs: Track A — Azure Storage Explorer (no coding)

title: Track B — Python
text:
Who this track is for
Developers who want to pull files from our Azure storage into scripts, notebooks, or pipelines. We’ll use Microsoft’s official Python library, azure-storage-blob, to connect, list what’s there, and download files.
If you’d rather just click to download, Track A does the same thing through a desktop app — no code needed.

title: Track B — Python
text:
What you’ll do

Set up — install Python and azure-storage-blob.
Authenticate — connect using the read-only SAS URL.
List & download — list containers/blobs and download files, with runnable code.

title: Track B — Python
text:
Before you start
Read Prerequisites & access and have your read-only SAS URL to hand (it already contains the storage account and container, so it’s the only thing you need):
https://PLACEHOLDER-STORAGE-ACCOUNT.blob.core.windows.net/PLACEHOLDER-CONTAINER-NAME?sp=rl&...&sig=PLACEHOLDER-SIGNATURE






Important



Your access is read-only (r + l = read + list). The code here only ever reads and downloads — calls that would write or delete will fail with a permissions error, by design.


→ Start with Set up.

title: Step 1 — Set up
text: You’ll need

title: Step 1 — Set up
text:
Python 3.8 or newer. Check with:
python --version
(On some systems it’s python3 --version.)
The azure-storage-blob package — Microsoft’s official library for Azure Blob storage.

title: Step 1 — Set up
text:
Install the library
It’s good practice to install into a virtual environment so this workshop’s packages don’t clash with your other projects:
# create and activate a virtual environment
python -m venv .venv

# macOS / Linux:
source .venv/bin/activate
# Windows (PowerShell):
.venv\Scripts\Activate.ps1

# install the Azure Blob storage library
pip install azure-storage-blob






Tip



We’ll only need azure-storage-blob for the SAS-token approach in this workshop. If you later switch to Azure AD / Entra ID auth, you’d also install azure-identity (for DefaultAzureCredential). You don’t need it today.

title: Step 1 — Set up
text:
Check it imports
Run this to confirm the install worked:
import azure.storage.blob
print(azure.storage.blob.VERSION)
If that prints a version number without error, you’re ready.

title: Step 1 — Set up
text:
Store your SAS URL safely
You’ll use the SAS URL in your code on the next page. Don’t hard-code it into scripts you commit — the URL is the key, so anyone who sees it can read our data until it expires. A simple, safe pattern is an environment variable:
# macOS / Linux
export AZURE_SAS_URL='https://PLACEHOLDER-STORAGE-ACCOUNT.blob.core.windows.net/PLACEHOLDER-CONTAINER-NAME?sp=rl&...&sig=PLACEHOLDER-SIGNATURE'

# Windows (PowerShell)
$env:AZURE_SAS_URL = 'https://PLACEHOLDER-STORAGE-ACCOUNT.blob.core.windows.net/PLACEHOLDER-CONTAINER-NAME?sp=rl&...&sig=PLACEHOLDER-SIGNATURE'
We’ll read it back with os.environ on the next page, so the secret never lives in your source code.






Tip



The bit of that URL after the ? is the SAS token. If you were ever given the token on its own instead of a full URL, store it the same way (e.g. in an AZURE_SAS_TOKEN variable) and the next page shows how to use either.


→ Next: Authenticate.

title: Step 2 — Authenticate
text:
Connect with your SAS URL
Here we connect to our storage using the read-only SAS URL from Prerequisites & access. Because the URL already points at our container, the library’s ContainerClient can use it directly.
→ Next: List & download.

title: Step 2 — Authenticate
text:
import os
from azure.storage.blob import ContainerClient

# Read the SAS URL from an environment variable (see the Set up page), so the
# secret never lives in your source code.
#   export AZURE_SAS_URL='https://...blob.core.windows.net/<container>?sp=rl&...&sig=...'
SAS_URL = os.environ["AZURE_SAS_URL"]

# The URL already includes the container and the credential, so this is all we need.
container = ContainerClient.from_container_url(SAS_URL)

print(f"Connected to container: {container.container_name}")






TipGiven just the SAS token (not a full URL)?



The token is the part after the ?. If that’s all you have, pair it with the account and container names and let BlobServiceClient build the connection:
import os
from azure.storage.blob import BlobServiceClient

ACCOUNT_NAME = "PLACEHOLDER-STORAGE-ACCOUNT"      # e.g. "imagostorage"
CONTAINER_NAME = "PLACEHOLDER-CONTAINER-NAME"     # e.g. "workshop-data"
SAS_TOKEN = os.environ["AZURE_SAS_TOKEN"]         # starts with "?sp=rl..."

service = BlobServiceClient(
    account_url=f"https://{ACCOUNT_NAME}.blob.core.windows.net",
    credential=SAS_TOKEN,
)
container = service.get_container_client(CONTAINER_NAME)








NoteUsing Azure AD instead?



If your team uses Entra ID rather than a SAS URL, install azure-identity and swap the credential — point a client at the container and pass the credential:
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.storage.blob import ContainerClient

container = ContainerClient(
    account_url="https://PLACEHOLDER-STORAGE-ACCOUNT.blob.core.windows.net",
    container_name="PLACEHOLDER-CONTAINER-NAME",
    credential=DefaultAzureCredential(),
)
For this workshop, stick with the SAS URL.

title: Step 3 — List & download
text:
List the files (blobs)
You’re connected (from Authenticate, you have a container client). Now let’s see what’s in the container and pull files down.
list_blobs() returns each blob’s name, size, last_modified, and more.

title: Step 3 — List & download
text:
# List every blob in the container.
print("Files in the container:")
for blob in container.list_blobs():
    size_kb = (blob.size or 0) / 1024
    print(f"  {blob.name}  ({size_kb:,.1f} KB)")







Tip



If the container has a lot of files, filter by a name prefix instead of listing everything — this is the equivalent of opening one “folder”:
for blob in container.list_blobs(name_starts_with="reports/"):
    print(blob.name)

title: Step 3 — List & download
text:
Download one file
from pathlib import Path

BLOB_NAME = "PLACEHOLDER-EXAMPLE-FILE"   # e.g. "sample-data.csv"
dest = Path("downloads") / BLOB_NAME
dest.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

blob_client = container.get_blob_client(BLOB_NAME)

with open(dest, "wb") as f:
    download = blob_client.download_blob()
    download.readinto(f)

print(f"Downloaded {BLOB_NAME} -> {dest}")
download_blob().readinto(f) streams the file straight to disk, so it works fine for large files without loading them all into memory.

title: Step 3 — List & download
section: Read a file’s contents directly (no saving)
text:
Read a file’s contents directly (no saving)
Sometimes you just want the data in memory — e.g. load a CSV into pandas:
data = container.get_blob_client("PLACEHOLDER-EXAMPLE-FILE").download_blob().readall()
print(f"Read {len(data):,} bytes")

# Example: straight into pandas (requires `pip install pandas`)
# import io, pandas as pd
# df = pd.read_csv(io.BytesIO(data))
# print(df.head())

title: Step 3 — List & download
text:
Download everything in the container
from pathlib import Path

out_dir = Path("downloads")

for blob in container.list_blobs():
    target = out_dir / blob.name
    target.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)   # preserve "folder" structure
    with open(target, "wb") as f:
        container.get_blob_client(blob.name).download_blob().readinto(f)
    print(f"Downloaded {blob.name}")

title: Step 3 — List & download
text:
A note on read-only
Everything above only reads. If you try a write or delete — for example container.upload_blob(...) or blob_client.delete_blob() — it will fail with a permissions error (403), because your SAS URL doesn’t grant those rights. That’s the intended safety net: you can’t change or remove our data.

title: Step 3 — List & download
text:
You’re done 🎉
You can now authenticate, list, and download from Azure storage in Python. Keep this page as a reference snippet bank.
